message:
feat(robot,dashboard): mail attention signals and snapshot bootstrap contract

br-fsoxa: Add mail attention signals to the Attention Feed
- Add PublishMailPending and PublishMailAckRequired methods
- Integrate into dashboard AgentMailInboxSummaryMsg handler
- Track seen message IDs to avoid duplicate signals

br-89wvt: Enhance snapshot bootstrap with cursor-bearing metadata
- Add Reason, EventCount, OldestTimestamp, LatestTimestamp to ReplayWindow
- Set Supported=false when feed is empty (no events to replay)
- Update tests for new bootstrap contract semantics
- Remove duplicate EventsOutput from attention_contract.go

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"br-7v4ns","title":"Attention Feed Phase 1b: Map existing bus/tracker/session signals into one normalized feed service","description":"# Why this bead exists
A journal alone is not useful if each producer speaks a different dialect. ntm already emits data from several places: event bus notifications, tracker state changes, session/pane lifecycle transitions, alerts, activity monitors, and other robot helpers. This bead makes those sources legible to a robot client through one internal mapping/query layer.

# Scope
1. Map existing tracker change types and bus events into the normalized envelope.
2. Decide which fields should be filled for each source: category, type, summary, details, source, and candidate `next_actions`.
3. Route session and pane lifecycle events through the same path used for replay and live subscription.
4. Create one shared query/subscription service so `--robot-events`, `--robot-digest`, wait conditions, and server streaming all consume the same underlying feed.
5. Keep raw output out of the high-level envelope unless it is genuinely required; the feed should tell an operator what changed, not dump pane text continuously.
6. Document unmapped or intentionally suppressed source events in code/tests so future contributors understand what was omitted and why.

# Design considerations
- Summary strings should be compact but concrete, because they are what an LLM will read most often.
- `details` should hold structured fields that later commands can use mechanically.
- `next_actions` must never point at imaginary commands; they should reference real robot-mode follow-ups such as `--robot-tail`, `--robot-context`, `--robot-status`, or the new feed surfaces.
- Avoid duplicating mapping logic in both CLI and server code; parity depends on one source of truth.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Mapping tests for tracker changes, bus events, session creation/removal, pane creation/removal, alerts, and activity changes.
- Tests that validate `next_actions` only reference real supported surfaces.
- Fixture coverage table that lists each input event family and the expected normalized output shape.
- Debug logging in tests for mapping misses or intentionally dropped events.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Existing ntm signals are representable through one normalized mapping path.
- [ ] Replay and live subscription go through the same mapping logic.
- [ ] Event summaries/details are specific enough to guide follow-up inspection without requiring raw logs.
- [ ] The implementation does not fork into separate CLI-only and server-only schemas.
- [ ] Unmapped or intentionally omitted signals are explicit rather than accidental.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":240,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:19.637988898Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T03:11:04.602754573Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T03:11:04.602174267Z","close_reason":"Added shared AttentionFeed publish helpers for tracker/logged/bus sources, live bus subscription, and mapping/service tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","events","mapping","phase-1","robot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-7v4ns","depends_on_id":"br-xmmaj","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:22.564595243Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-7zdzv","title":"PHASE 0: Capture TUI Baselines via VHS Inspector","description":"# Phase 0: Capture TUI Baselines

## Why This Must Come First
Every subsequent TUI change needs a visual reference point. Without baselines, we cannot
objectively verify that upgrades improve the UI without introducing regressions. The TUI
Inspector infrastructure already exists — we just need to run it and store the artifacts.

## What To Do

### Step 1: Run doctor check
  bash /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/doctor_tui_inspector.sh
Verify: vhs, ttyd, ffmpeg all present and functional. Log the output.

### Step 2: Build NTM binary
  go build -o /tmp/ntm_baseline ./cmd/ntm
Record the binary size and build time.

### Step 3: Capture all 5 profiles
  bash /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/run_ntm_tui_suite.sh \
    --profiles ntm-dashboard-empty,ntm-dashboard-help,ntm-dashboard-navigate,ntm-dashboard-wide,ntm-palette \
    --suite-name "baseline_pre_upgrade"
Log: each profile's exit code, video duration, snapshot status.

### Step 4: Run native VHS tapes
  cd /data/projects/ntm/testdata/vhs && for tape in *.tape; do vhs "$tape" 2>&1 | tee /tmp/vhs_${tape%.tape}.log; done

### Step 5: Run existing TUI unit tests as baseline
  go test -v -short ./internal/tui/... 2>&1 | tee /tmp/tui_tests_baseline.log
Record: test count, pass/fail, duration. This is the baseline test count we must not regress.

### Step 6: Archive baseline artifacts
Store in /tmp/tui_inspector/suites/baseline_pre_upgrade/
Create a manifest: baseline_manifest.json with binary size, test count, capture metadata.

## Unit Test Baseline
Run and record all existing tests:
  go test -v -count=1 -short ./internal/tui/... 2>&1 | tee /tmp/tui_test_baseline.log
  go test -v -count=1 -short ./internal/cli/... 2>&1 | tee /tmp/cli_test_baseline.log
Count passing tests. This number MUST NOT decrease in any subsequent phase.

## Logging
- Log doctor check output to /tmp/tui_inspector/baseline_doctor.log
- Log each VHS capture stdout/stderr to /tmp/tui_inspector/baseline_capture_*.log
- Log test run output to /tmp/tui_test_baseline.log
- Create /tmp/tui_inspector/baseline_summary.json with:
  { "binary_size": N, "test_count": N, "test_pass": N, "capture_count": 5, "all_pass": bool }

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 5 TUI Inspector profiles capture successfully (exit code 0 in run_meta.json)
- All 5 snapshots extracted (snapshot_exists: true in each run_meta.json)
- Suite manifest shows 5/5 passed
- All existing TUI unit tests pass
- Baseline artifacts archived and accessible
- baseline_summary.json created with all metrics

## Files Involved
- /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/capture_ntm_tui.sh
- /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/run_ntm_tui_suite.sh
- /dp/tui_inspector/scripts/doctor_tui_inspector.sh
- /dp/tui_inspector/profiles/ntm-*.env (5 profiles)
- /data/projects/ntm/testdata/vhs/*.tape (5 tapes)

 {"id":"br-815ez","title":"Attention Feed Phase 3a: Build the digest engine that turns feed noise into token-efficient deltas","description":"# Why this bead exists
A frontier LLM does not usually want the raw event stream first. Most of the time it wants a compact answer to "what changed that I should care about?" This bead builds the digest engine that turns feed noise into token-efficient deltas while preserving the ability to drill into raw events when needed.

# Scope
1. Coalesce recent feed activity into a compact digest grouped by `actionable`, `interesting`, `background`, and suppressed counts.
2. Generate a stable summary string that is cheap for an operator LLM to consume.
3. Preserve cursor boundaries so the digest can be chained cleanly in a long-running tending loop.
4. Retain event references/details needed for targeted follow-up rather than forcing a fresh snapshot.
5. Avoid aggressive magic. Coalescing should be rule-based and testable, not mysterious.
6. Establish explicit suppression rules for repetitive output churn, lifecycle noise, and redundant alert spam so later changes do not quietly make digests unusably noisy.

# Design considerations
- Repeated passive output should usually be suppressed, not surfaced one-by-one.
- Important coordination or stall signals must survive coalescing.
- The digest should remain predictable enough that agents can build habits around it.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Tests for suppression of noisy output bursts.
- Tests that important signals survive coalescing.
- Golden tests for summary stability and bucket assignment.
- Benchmarks or stress tests for digesting large bursts without pathological allocations.
- Debug logging in tests that shows which source events were surfaced, coalesced, or suppressed and why.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The digest separates action-required changes from passive background churn.
- [ ] Summary strings remain compact but concrete.
- [ ] Suppression/coalescing rules are deterministic and testable.
- [ ] Cursor boundaries remain explicit.
- [ ] Digest noise-control behavior is diagnosable from test output.","notes":"Digest engine implemented in internal/robot/attention_feed.go with coverage in internal/robot/attention_feed_test.go. -{"id":"br-89wvt","title":"Attention Feed Phase 2a1: Add cursor-bearing snapshot bootstrap and resync metadata","description":"# Why this bead exists
The operator loop cannot start cleanly unless snapshot bootstrap semantics are correct on their own. In the current plan, snapshot bootstrap and snapshot ergonomics are bundled together, which makes the cold-start contract harder to reason about and blocks downstream work on features that only need a correct cursor handoff. This bead isolates the mechanical bootstrap contract first.

# Scope
1. Extend `--robot-snapshot` so it always returns a canonical cursor boundary suitable for immediate handoff into replay/digest/wait/attention flows.
2. Add explicit replay-window metadata such as the oldest replayable cursor, whether replay is currently supported, and what command the caller should use when replay cannot continue.
3. Keep this bead focused on correctness of bootstrap/resync semantics, not on higher-level summary ergonomics.
4. Ensure the bootstrap payload remains consistent with existing robot JSON semantics and does not introduce an incompatible one-off shape.
5. Make the snapshot response precise enough that a caller can reason about replay availability without guessing.

# Design considerations
- Bootstrap correctness should not be blocked on the later user-experience polish of snapshot summaries.
- This bead is about making the cursor boundary mechanically trustworthy.
- Any field added here should be stable and reusable by the later snapshot-ergonomics bead rather than thrown away.

# Likely touch points
- `internal/robot/robot.go`
- snapshot-producing robot helpers
- `internal/cli/root.go`
- `internal/output/`

# Detailed verification expectations
- Golden tests for snapshot responses with and without sessions/panes.
- Scenario tests that hand the returned cursor directly into `--robot-events`, `--robot-digest`, `--robot-wait`, and `--robot-attention`.
- Expired-cursor tests proving snapshot is the documented and working resync path.
- Transcript-style scenario coverage for bootstrap -> replay -> forced expiration -> resnapshot.
- Detailed logs in tests that print cursor handoff, oldest replayable cursor, and resync hints when expectations fail.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `--robot-snapshot` returns a canonical cursor boundary suitable for downstream robot surfaces.
- [ ] Replay-window / resync metadata are explicit enough for agents to act mechanically.
- [ ] The bootstrap contract is correct and testable independent of later summary ergonomics.
- [ ] Automated tests and transcript scenarios cover bootstrap and resync behavior thoroughly.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":120,"created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:35.005533156Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T02:49:44.704033631Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","bootstrap","cli","phase-2","robot","snapshot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-89wvt","depends_on_id":"br-7v4ns","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:39.442716860Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"br-91gti","title":"Attention Feed Phase 3e: Add operator/debug attention profiles and low-noise presets","description":"# Why this bead exists
The core attention-feed surfaces can be mechanically correct and still feel awkward in real use if every operator has to rediscover the right filter mix by hand. Earlier design work explicitly called for two qualitatively different usage modes: a low-noise tending mode and a high-fidelity debugging mode. That is product behavior, not incidental polish, and it needs to be designed as a first-class contract.

# Scope
1. Add explicit profile or preset support for the attention-feed surfaces so callers can ask for a named noise model instead of reconstructing the same filter bundle every time.
2. At minimum, support an `operator` profile optimized for steady-state tending and a `debug` profile optimized for exhaustive visibility.
3. Define one resolution algorithm for profiles so `--robot-events`, `--robot-digest`, `--robot-wait`, and `--robot-attention` all interpret the same profile name the same way.
4. Preserve explicit filters as the source of maximum control. Profiles are presets, not replacements, and precedence rules must be explicit and machine-testable.
5. Ensure network transport behavior remains coherent when profiles are exposed through HTTP, SSE, or WebSocket paths.
6. Make profile availability and effective behavior discoverable through `--robot-help`, `--robot-capabilities`, dashboard/terse views, and tests so humans and agents do not learn different defaults.

# Design considerations
- Profiles must reduce cognitive load, not add another ambiguous layer.
- The system should be able to log the resolved effective filter set so debugging profile behavior never depends on guesswork.
- If one profile is the default, that choice must be explicit, justified, and covered by tests.
- Wait/attention wake behavior under a profile must be as precise as events/digest output under that same profile.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Unit tests for profile expansion and effective-filter resolution.
- Tests for `operator` and `debug` behavior across events, digest, wait, and attention.
- Integration tests for profile parity across CLI and network surfaces where profiles are supported.
- Precedence tests for profile plus explicit filters, including contradictory and narrowing combinations.
- Golden tests for help/capabilities output that describe the available profiles and their intent.
- Transcript scenarios that compare low-noise tending against high-fidelity debugging on the same underlying event stream.
- Detailed logs in tests that show the requested profile, resolved effective filters, transport/command surface, and the reason an item was included, suppressed, or downgraded.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `operator` and `debug` are first-class, discoverable behavior modes rather than folklore.
- [ ] Profile resolution is consistent across the core attention-feed surfaces.
- [ ] Explicit filters still work and precedence is unambiguous.
- [ ] Profile behavior is demonstrated by unit, integration, and transcript-style scenarios.
- [ ] Future users can learn the intended noise model from ntm itself rather than an external plan document.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":150,"created_at":"2026-03-21T01:05:01.028426698Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T01:12:34.061901159Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","ergonomics","phase-3","profiles","robot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-91gti","depends_on_id":"br-6tzh9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:05:03.329832480Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-91gti","depends_on_id":"br-7kigk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:27.924032425Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-91gti","depends_on_id":"br-kpvhy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:05:02.898819586Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-91gti","depends_on_id":"br-t540i","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:28.196967876Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-91gti","depends_on_id":"br-tsqxo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:28.415680461Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-9bmtl","title":"Attention Feed Phase 4b1: Build comprehensive unit and integration coverage for the new operator loop","description":"# Why this bead exists
The earlier test bead covered the core feed, but it still left too much room for user-facing summary surfaces to be under-tested. If dashboard/terse and profile behavior are part of the product contract, they need deterministic automated coverage, not just manual spot-checking.

# Scope
1. Add deterministic unit tests for envelope serialization, cursor allocation, replay windows, eviction, mapping, derived signals, snapshot bootstrap, digest coalescing, wait conditions, profile resolution, and summary/view-model generation.
2. Add integration tests for CLI and server parity so the same underlying event looks materially the same across command and transport surfaces.
3. Add deterministic tests for dashboard/terse summary output, including quiet, interesting, action-required, degraded, and profile-specific cases.
4. Add command-chain tests for snapshot -> events/digest/wait/attention handoff where a full transcript-style e2e harness is not required.
5. Keep this bead focused on deterministic unit/integration verification; long-form transcript scenarios live in the paired e2e bead.

# Logging / artifact expectations
- Failed tests should log cursor ranges, matched conditions, suppression decisions, active profiles, transport mode, and summary bucket state.
- Golden/parity tests should emit diff-friendly diagnostics when payloads or terse/dashboard summaries drift.
- Integration tests should capture enough structured context to explain parity failures without immediate manual reproduction.
- Any shared summary/view-model regressions should print both the underlying signal snapshot and the rendered summary so future maintainers can see where divergence started.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Core feed, snapshot bootstrap, digest, wait, attention, transport, profile, and dashboard/terse summary semantics are covered automatically at unit/integration level.
- [ ] Important failure modes such as expired cursor, unsupported replay window, partial observability, and contradictory profile/filter combinations have explicit automated coverage.
- [ ] Logs are detailed enough to debug failures without guesswork.

# Test commands that must pass before closing this bead
- `go build ./cmd/ntm`
- `go test -short ./...`
- package-targeted tests for `internal/robot/...`, `internal/serve/...`, `internal/events/...`, `internal/tracker/...`, and any dashboard/output packages touched by the feature","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":240,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:21.798002184Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T01:12:36.121519215Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","phase-4","robot","tests","verification"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-9bmtl","depends_on_id":"br-6tzh9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:29.031845838Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-9bmtl","depends_on_id":"br-7kigk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:29.394483660Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-9bmtl","depends_on_id":"br-91gti","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:29.447157567Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-9bmtl","depends_on_id":"br-auag6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:29.710446136Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-9bmtl","depends_on_id":"br-slg9g","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:57:17.871290905Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-9bmtl","depends_on_id":"br-t540i","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:29.761115149Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-9bmtl","depends_on_id":"br-tsqxo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:28.667513894Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-a1ti3","title":"Overlay-Feed T7: Verify end-to-end overlay-feed integration and document invariants","description":"# Why this bead exists
The overlay/feed branch needs its own final verification gate for the same reason the core attention-feed initiative does: the question is not whether each sub-bead sounds plausible, but whether the whole human/agent handoff loop actually works coherently and leaves behind understandable artifacts for future maintainers.

# Scope
1. Verify the overlay/feed cold-start path when the feed is unavailable or empty.
2. Verify the warm path where real attention items, badge state, and overlay summaries agree.
3. Verify zoom/dismiss continuity events and cursor hints.
4. Verify cursor-targeted human handoff from an agent-facing attention response into the overlay.
5. Verify `assign --watch` workflow discoverability and overlay entry.
6. Confirm all documented invariants and non-goals remain true.
7. Confirm the overlay branch still degrades honestly when the feed is unavailable.
8. Leave behind a concise record of what commands/scenarios were run and where the most useful artifacts live.

# Invariants that must hold
- The overlay never becomes a second event source of truth.
- Human-originated overlay events remain background-level continuity signals, not synthetic emergencies.
- The feed can function without the overlay, and the overlay can degrade gracefully without the feed.
- Cursor identity used in overlay handoff remains the same cursor identity used by the attention feed.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Transcript-style e2e scenarios for cold start, warm path, zoom-return loop, agent handoff, watch-mode integration, and degraded/no-feed behavior.
- TUI or screenshot artifacts where they materially help future debugging.
- Diff-friendly diagnostics that identify which invariant failed when a scenario breaks.
- Logs that record cursor values, selected focus targets, emitted human-originated events, overlay state, and degraded-mode markers.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The overlay/feed integration behaves coherently across its main happy paths and degraded paths.
- [ ] The documented invariants are verified, not just asserted.
- [ ] Useful artifacts remain behind for future debugging.

 {"id":"br-fsoxa","title":"Attention Feed Phase 1d1: Add Agent Mail unread and ack-required attention signals","description":"# Why this bead exists
Unread and ack-required Agent Mail changes are some of the clearest reasons a tending agent should wake up. In the previous plan, they were bundled with unrelated coordination concerns. This bead isolates the mail-specific signal work so it can be implemented, tested, and reasoned about independently.

# Scope
1. Surface `mail_pending` when new relevant unread mail appears for an active session/operator surface.
2. Surface `mail_ack_required` when ack-required mail specifically needs human/agent attention.
3. Carry enough structured detail for follow-up: thread/message identifiers when available, ack requirement, sender, and suggested mechanical next actions.
4. Keep the signals subordinate to Agent Mail as the source of truth; ntm is surfacing attention, not replacing the inbox.
5. Define honest absence semantics when Agent Mail visibility is unavailable or partial.

# Design considerations
- Mail attention is high-value and should not be blocked on unrelated conflict-detection work.
- The distinction between unread mail and ack-required mail matters operationally and should not be blurred.
- These signals should point operators back to Agent Mail or existing ntm surfaces, not invent a parallel message system.

# Likely touch points
- `internal/agentmail/`
- shared feed/signal logic
- `internal/robot/` integration points

# Detailed verification expectations
- Tests for unread vs ack-required mail conditions.
- Tests for relevant metadata extraction and `next_actions` hints.
- Tests for honest degradation when Agent Mail visibility is missing or incomplete.
- Transcript scenarios showing a waiting operator waking specifically on ack-required mail.
- Detailed logs in tests that record the mail source state and why a signal was or was not emitted.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `mail_pending` and `mail_ack_required` are implemented and clearly differentiated.
- [ ] The signals point back to the real source of truth instead of duplicating inbox state.
- [ ] Partial observability is represented honestly.
- [ ] Mail attention behavior is covered by detailed automated tests and transcript scenarios.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":180,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:20.055629416Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T08:15:45.160467502Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T08:15:45.159941515Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","coordination","phase-1","robot","signals"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-fsoxa","depends_on_id":"br-04qv0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:23.183970560Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-h3z1b","title":"Phase 1b: Wire Harmonica Spring Animations for Toast Notifications","description":"# Wire Harmonica Spring Animations for Toast Notifications

## Background
charmbracelet/harmonica v0.2.0 is in go.mod with ZERO imports. Toast system
(components/toast.go, 209 LOC) already has a working Tick() method (line 79) that prunes
expired toasts and cleans dedup entries. We are EXTENDING Tick(), not creating it.

## ACTUAL Toast API (verified from source)
  type ToastManager struct {
      toasts []Toast
      seen   map[string]time.Time
  }
  func NewToastManager() *ToastManager
  func (tm *ToastManager) Push(toast Toast)        // line 52
  func (tm *ToastManager) Tick()                   // line 79 — ALREADY EXISTS
  func (tm *ToastManager) Count() int              // line 102
  func (tm *ToastManager) RenderToasts(maxWidth int) string  // line 108

  type Toast struct {
      ID, Message string
      Severity    ToastSeverity
      CreatedAt   time.Time
      TTL         time.Duration
  }

## Implementation Plan — EXTEND existing Tick(), don't replace

### 1. Add harmonica import
  import "github.com/charmbracelet/harmonica"

### 2. Add spring fields to Toast struct
  type Toast struct {
      // existing fields preserved
      ID, Message string
      Severity    ToastSeverity
      CreatedAt   time.Time
      TTL         time.Duration
      // NEW spring fields
      offsetX    float64
      offsetXVel float64
      spring     harmonica.Spring
      dismissed  bool
  }

### 3. Modify Push() to initialize spring
  func (tm *ToastManager) Push(toast Toast) {
      if styles.ReducedMotionEnabled() {
          toast.offsetX = 0.0
      } else {
          toast.spring = harmonica.NewSpring(harmonica.FPS(60), 6.0, 0.4)
          toast.offsetX = 40.0  // start offscreen right
      }
      // ... existing dedup logic preserved
  }

### 4. EXTEND Tick() — add spring updates before existing expiry logic
  func (tm *ToastManager) Tick() {
      // NEW: Update spring positions
      if !styles.ReducedMotionEnabled() {
          for i := range tm.toasts {
              t := &tm.toasts[i]
              target := 0.0
              if t.dismissed { target = 60.0 }
              t.offsetX, t.offsetXVel = t.spring.Update(t.offsetX, t.offsetXVel, target)
          }
          // Remove dismissed toasts that slid fully offscreen
          tm.removeSettledDismissed()
      }
      // EXISTING: Prune expired toasts (preserved from line 79)
      now := time.Now()
      active := tm.toasts[:0]
      for _, t := range tm.toasts {
          if now.Sub(t.CreatedAt) < t.TTL { active = append(active, t) }
      }
      tm.toasts = active
      // EXISTING: Clean old dedup entries (preserved)
      ...
  }

### 5. Modify RenderToasts() — apply offsetX
  In rendering, if offsetX > 0, shift toast rightward (or truncate from right).

### 6. Add IsAnimating() method
  func (tm *ToastManager) IsAnimating() bool {
      for _, t := range tm.toasts {
          if math.Abs(t.offsetX) > 0.5 || math.Abs(t.offsetXVel) > 0.1 { return true }
          if t.dismissed { return true }
      }
      return false
  }

### 7. Add Dismiss(id string) method
  func (tm *ToastManager) Dismiss(id string) {
      for i := range tm.toasts { if tm.toasts[i].ID == id { tm.toasts[i].dismissed = true } }
  }

## Unit Tests (REQUIRED)

### Add to existing toast tests (or new file)
  func TestToastSpringInit(t *testing.T) {
      tm := NewToastManager()
      tm.Push(Toast{ID: "test", Message: "Hi", Severity: SeverityInfo, TTL: 4*time.Second})
      if len(tm.toasts) == 0 { t.Fatal("no toast") }
      if tm.toasts[0].offsetX != 40.0 { t.Errorf("expected 40, got %f", tm.toasts[0].offsetX) }
  }

  func TestToastSpringSettles(t *testing.T) {
      tm := NewToastManager()
      tm.Push(Toast{ID: "test", Message: "Hi", Severity: SeverityInfo, TTL: 60*time.Second})
      for i := 0; i < 200; i++ { tm.Tick() }
      if math.Abs(tm.toasts[0].offsetX) > 0.5 { t.Errorf("not settled: %f", tm.toasts[0].offsetX) }
  }

  func TestToastDismissSlideOut(t *testing.T) {
      tm := NewToastManager()
      tm.Push(Toast{ID: "test", Message: "Hi", Severity: SeverityInfo, TTL: 60*time.Second})
      for i := 0; i < 200; i++ { tm.Tick() } // settle
      tm.Dismiss("test")
      for i := 0; i < 200; i++ { tm.Tick() } // slide out
      // Toast should be removed after sliding offscreen
  }

  func TestToastReducedMotionInstant(t *testing.T) {
      t.Setenv("NTM_REDUCE_MOTION", "1")
      tm := NewToastManager()
      tm.Push(Toast{ID: "test", Message: "Hi", Severity: SeverityInfo, TTL: 4*time.Second})
      if tm.toasts[0].offsetX != 0.0 { t.Error("reduced motion: instant position") }
  }

  func TestToastIsAnimating(t *testing.T) {
      tm := NewToastManager()
      if tm.IsAnimating() { t.Error("empty: not animating") }
      tm.Push(Toast{ID: "test", Message: "Hi", Severity: SeverityInfo, TTL: 60*time.Second})
      if !tm.IsAnimating() { t.Error("fresh toast: should animate") }
      for i := 0; i < 300; i++ { tm.Tick() }
      if tm.IsAnimating() { t.Error("settled: not animating") }
  }

  func TestToastExistingTickPreserved(t *testing.T) {
      // Verify existing expiry behavior still works
      tm := NewToastManager()
      tm.Push(Toast{ID: "test", Message: "Hi", Severity: SeverityInfo, TTL: 0}) // instant expire
      tm.Tick()
      if tm.Count() != 0 { t.Error("expired toast should be removed") }
  }

  func BenchmarkToastTick4(b *testing.B) {
      tm := NewToastManager()
      for i := 0; i < 4; i++ {
          tm.Push(Toast{ID: fmt.Sprintf("t%d",i), Message: "msg", Severity: SeverityInfo, TTL: 60*time.Second})
      }
      b.ResetTimer()
      for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ { tm.Tick() }
  }

## E2E / VHS Verification
Run ntm-dashboard-empty profile capture. Review .mp4 for smooth slide-in.

## Logging
- Log spring init: "toast %s: spring stiffness=6.0 damping=0.4 startOffset=40"
- Debug per-tick: "toast %s: offset=%.2f vel=%.2f" (when NTM_DEBUG=1)
- Log dismiss: "toast %s: dismissed, sliding out"
- Log settle: "toast %s: spring settled"

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] harmonica imported (FIRST usage in entire codebase)
- [ ] Existing Toast struct fields preserved (new fields added)
- [ ] Existing Tick() behavior preserved (expiry logic unchanged)
- [ ] Push() initializes spring state
- [ ] Toasts slide in from right with overshoot
- [ ] Dismiss() triggers slide-out animation
- [ ] ReducedMotion: instant position, no animation
- [ ] IsAnimating() correctly reports state
- [ ] All 7 new tests pass
- [ ] All existing toast tests pass
- [ ] Benchmark < 1μs per Tick() for 4 toasts
- [ ] VHS video shows smooth animation

## Files to Modify
- internal/tui/components/toast.go (extend struct, Tick(), add methods)
- internal/tui/dashboard/dashboard.go (fast tick when IsAnimating())

## Risk: LOW — EXTENDING existing code, not replacing.

## PARALLEL EXECUTION NOTE
This bead and Phase 1a (br-08fgf) both modify dashboard.go. Changes are in
NON-OVERLAPPING sections (1a: RunWithOptions + MouseMsg case; 1b: DashboardTickMsg
handler). If running in parallel agents, use Agent Mail file reservations to
coordinate, or merge sequentially. Git should auto-merge cleanly.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":120,"created_at":"2026-03-20T22:25:56.189050208Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T02:58:06.667616431Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T02:58:06.667165918Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["animation","harmonica","phase-1","toast","tui","upgrade"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-h3z1b","depends_on_id":"br-7zdzv","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-20T22:32:52.925195346Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-h3z1b","depends_on_id":"br-phdna","type":"related","created_at":"2026-03-20T22:34:45.451190259Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-ht8jy","title":"Overlay-Feed T4: Support --attention-cursor flag to open overlay pre-focused on specific event","description":"# Why this bead exists
A tending agent sometimes needs to bring a human directly to the attention item that triggered a wake-up. If the overlay can only open in a generic unfocused state, the human still has to hunt around and the handoff loses most of its value. This bead makes cursor-targeted overlay opening a first-class bridge between agent awareness and human awareness.

# Scope
1. Add an `--attention-cursor` path for opening the dashboard/overlay pre-focused on a specific attention item or the nearest surviving item.
2. Ensure the overlay focuses the attention panel and highlights the targeted item when it is still available.
3. Degrade honestly when the cursor is expired or no longer available by opening to the best current frontier rather than pretending exact continuity exists.
4. Provide a mechanical command shape that later robot-mode surfaces can emit directly as a suggested human-handoff action.
5. Keep cursor semantics aligned with the same namespace used by the attention feed and `--robot-attention` responses.

# Design considerations
- This feature is only useful if the cursor namespace is truly shared; any fork here would be a serious design bug.
- Focus/highlight behavior should make the handoff obvious without becoming visually obnoxious.
- Degraded behavior must be explicit so humans and agents do not infer false precision.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Unit tests for argument handling, focus selection, and cursor-found versus cursor-expired behavior.
- TUI snapshot/golden tests for focused/highlighted attention-panel rendering.
- Integration tests that prove robot attention responses can point to this surface mechanically.
- Transcript scenarios showing an agent wake, a suggested overlay command, and a human landing on the intended item or best-effort fallback.
- Detailed logs in tests that print requested cursor, located cursor, selected focus target, and degraded fallback choice.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Cursor-targeted overlay opening exists and uses the same cursor namespace as the feed.
- [ ] The overlay focuses/highlights the intended attention item when possible.
- [ ] Expired or missing cursors degrade honestly and intelligibly.
- [ ] Automated tests and transcript artifacts make the handoff behavior easy to verify.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:07:58.617678724Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T08:13:12.744494987Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T08:13:12.743239544Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","dashboard","overlay","phase-2","robot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-ht8jy","depends_on_id":"br-5u235","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:10:28.434468366Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-ht8jy","depends_on_id":"br-abp0s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:10:29.400596235Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-ht8jy","depends_on_id":"br-x66gc","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:10:38.433829433Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"br-kpvhy","title":"Attention Feed Phase 2b: Ship --robot-events as the raw replay/feed surface","description":"# Why this bead exists
Robot clients need one raw, replayable, machine-oriented surface that answers "what happened since cursor N?" without forcing a full snapshot every time. `--robot-events` should be that surface.

# Scope
1. Add a canonical `--robot-events` command in robot mode.
2. Support cursor-based replay, filtering, and an obvious streaming/follow mode when appropriate.
3. Follow existing robot conventions for exit codes, `success`, `timestamp`, and structured error payloads.
4. Return cursor metadata in a way that makes cursor chaining straightforward for agent consumers.
5. Keep the interface simple and enumerable. Prefer explicit filters (`--session`, `--panes`, `--type`, `--category`, `--actionability`, `--since-cursor`, `--limit`) over a clever DSL.
6. Ensure the command's cursor/filter semantics line up exactly with the snapshot bootstrap path and later digest/wait/attention surfaces.
7. Make follow-mode behavior explicit about how quiet periods are represented, including whether the command stays silent, emits periodic liveness markers, or delegates liveness expectations entirely to the caller.

# Design considerations
- This command should be useful both for one-shot replay and long-lived operator loops.
- If a cursor is expired, the response must say so clearly and tell the caller to resync via snapshot.
- The command should not require a consumer to understand server internals or websocket topics.
- Quiet follow-mode behavior must be documented well enough that agents do not mistake a healthy idle stream for a broken one.

# Detailed verification expectations
- CLI golden tests for replay, filter, streaming/follow mode, quiet-period behavior, and expired-cursor responses.
- Tests for parity with the underlying normalized feed service.
- Transcript-style Go scenario coverage showing: snapshot -> events replay -> additional follow-mode wake.
- Go-based e2e harness coverage that captures stdout/stderr/log artifacts for raw event replay, quiet follow-mode, and expiration handling.
- Detailed failure logging that shows requested cursor, delivered cursor range, filter set, quiet-period behavior, and resync hint when assertions fail.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `--robot-events` exists as the raw replay/feed surface.
- [ ] Cursor chaining is obvious from the response shape.
- [ ] Structured filtering exists without introducing a bespoke query language.
- [ ] Error cases such as expired cursors or invalid filters are explicit and machine-actionable.
- [ ] Snapshot/bootstrap semantics and events replay semantics match exactly.
- [ ] Follow-mode quiet-period semantics are explicit enough that operators can distinguish healthy idleness from broken behavior.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":180,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:20.262903165Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T01:25:32.586758647Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","cli","events","phase-2","robot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-kpvhy","depends_on_id":"br-04qv0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:23.509563887Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-kpvhy","depends_on_id":"br-89wvt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:27.499979662Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"br-la5zy","title":"Overlay-Feed T5: Integrate F12 overlay toggle with assign --watch mode","description":"# Why this bead exists
`assign --watch` is one of the most likely places a human operator will sit while ntm is making decisions continuously. If overlay access is awkward there, one of the most important operational workflows remains disconnected from the new attention surfaces. This bead makes the watch workflow discover and use the overlay in a way that is actually useful, not just cosmetically bound.

# Scope
1. Detect when `assign --watch` is running inside the monitored tmux session and provide an explicit, one-time overlay hint in that context.
2. Ensure overlay binding/setup is present when the workflow can genuinely use it.
3. Make the hint and workflow point toward the attention-aware overlay experience rather than a generic dashboard detour.
4. Keep the behavior quiet and context-sensitive so remote/non-tmux usage does not get confusing noise.
5. Leave the watch loop semantics themselves unchanged; this is discoverability and ergonomics, not an assignment-engine rewrite.

# Design considerations
- This workflow should not advertise attention integration before the overlay attention panel exists.
- The hint should feel like a useful affordance, not nagware.
- Any auto-setup must stay consistent with the existing overlay binding logic rather than inventing a second path.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Unit tests for environment detection and hint emission/non-emission.
- Tests for overlay binding setup behavior when the binding already exists versus when it must be installed.
- Transcript scenarios showing `assign --watch` inside and outside the target tmux session.
- Detailed logs in tests that print session context, tmux detection result, whether the hint was emitted, and whether binding setup ran.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `assign --watch` surfaces overlay access only in the contexts where it is genuinely useful.
- [ ] The workflow points users toward the attention-aware overlay rather than a generic dashboard path.
- [ ] Binding setup remains aligned with existing overlay behavior.
- [ ] Automated tests and transcript artifacts make regressions easy to diagnose.","notes":"Implementation note: Part 1 (F12 hint + auto-setup) can be implemented immediately — it has no dependency on the Attention Feed and only touches existing overlay infrastructure.  {"id":"br-mamo3","title":"Attention Feed Phase 4b2: Build transcript-style e2e operator-loop scenarios and artifact logging","description":"# Why this bead exists
Transcript-style operator-loop scenarios are too important to leave implicit, and they now need to cover the human-facing summary surfaces as well as the raw robot loop. This bead exists to build the long-form scenario harnesses and artifact logging that prove the full user experience actually works end to end.

# Scope
1. Build Go-based e2e scenarios under `e2e/` for the canonical operator loop:
   - cold-start snapshot then cursor handoff
   - raw event replay after snapshot
   - wake on stalled pane
   - wake on ack-required mail
   - wake on reservation/file conflict
   - `--robot-attention` repeating cleanly across multiple wake cycles
   - resync behavior after expired cursor or process restart
